config.title: Spandau Berlin: FAQ
config.subtitle: Berlin’s real old town and its Renaissance fortress: tickets, opening quirks, getting there on an AB ticket, and what is actually worth your time.

items.a: Yes, if you care about the Berlin that existed before the 20th century. Spandau has a complete moated Renaissance fortress, the oldest building in Berlin, a genuine medieval street plan and a full-height stretch of 14th-century town wall. It works best as a relaxed half-day: the Citadel first, then the old town, then the Kolk quarter. If your Berlin list is mostly Mitte museums and Wall history, it is a pleasant contrast rather than a must.

items.q: How do I get to Spandau from central Berlin?
items.a: Take the S3 or S9 from Alexanderplatz or Hauptbahnhof to Berlin-Spandau station, about 30 minutes, or the U7 to Zitadelle for the fortress or Altstadt Spandau for the old town. All of Spandau is inside fare zone B, so a normal Berlin AB ticket covers it. On the 2026 construction weekends of 24 to 27 July and 31 July to 3 August the Stadtbahn S-Bahn is suspended, so use the U7 on those dates.

items.q: How much does the Spandau Citadel cost and when is it open?
items.a: Admission is 4.50 euros, reduced 2.50, family ticket 10 euros, children under six free, and the ticket includes the museums, the exhibitions and the Juliusturm climb. Opening hours are Friday to Wednesday 10:00 to 17:00 and Thursday 13:00 to 20:00, with last entry 30 minutes before closing. Hours can shift on summer concert days, so check the official site before you go.

Yes, the climb is included in the normal Citadel ticket. The Juliusturm is the keep of the medieval castle, built in the early 13th century, which makes it the oldest surviving building in Berlin. The staircase is a tight spiral and the platform looks across the moat, the old town and the Havel. From 1874 to 1919 the tower stored 120 million gold marks of French war reparations, which is why Germans still call a state reserve a Juliusturm.

items.q: Is Spandau really older than Berlin?
items.a: By the documents, yes. Spandau received its town charter around 1232, while Berlin first appears in surviving records in 1237. Spandau only became part of Berlin in 1920, and the district has never quite forgiven it: locals still talk about going to Berlin when they head into the centre.

No. The Allied prison where Rudolf Hess was the last inmate was demolished in 1987, deliberately, so it could never become a shrine. The rubble was ground down and scattered, and a British forces shopping centre was later built on the site. There is nothing to see, and the district’s real history is the 800-year-old town itself.

If your Berlin list is mostly Mitte museums and Wall history, it is a pleasant contrast rather than a must.
